location.formative: Seattle, WA — Central District, Garfield High School
location.career: New York, NY → Los Angeles, CA

examples: Sinatra at the Sands live album (1966)
theory_nodes.description: Foundational cadential motion in Western tonal harmony. Jones built his entire arranging vocabulary on ii-V-I progressions decorated with chromatic approach chords and inner-voice leading.

examples: Sinatra arrangements — brass voicings with bII7 replacing V7
Walking bass reharmonization in Basie charts
Fly Me to the Moon harmonic decoration
theory_nodes.description: Replacing a dominant seventh chord with the dominant seventh a tritone away, exploiting the shared tritone interval between the 3rd and 7th of both chords. Jones used this as a signature reharmonization technique in big-band arranging.

examples: Section writing: brass/reed/rhythm as architectural modules
Voicing techniques: close-position sax soli, open brass voicings
theory_nodes.description: Treating each instrument section as a module in a system. The arrangement defines interfaces between modules. Jones produced orchestral complexity from 13-17 musicians through intelligent voicing, doubling, and dynamic orchestration — the Amiga Principle in musical form.

examples: Off the Wall — jazz-disco fusion via Mixolydian mode and horn arrangements
Thriller — pop-R&B-rock synthesis across 9 tracks in distinct genres
Bad — R&B-hip-hop bridge with jazz-inflected production
theory_nodes.description: Jones demonstrated that jazz harmony could function inside pop and R&B production without losing theoretical depth or commercial accessibility. This is architectural translation, not fusion — theory made invisible while remaining structurally essential.

examples: In the Heat of the Night (1967) — leitmotif and through-composed narrative scoring
Roots (1977) — longitudinal musical history spanning centuries
The Color Purple (1985) — dramatic arc mapping
theory_nodes.description: Through-composed film scoring as continuous musical narrative synchronized to dramatic arc. Leitmotif technique organizes the score as a system of relationships. Requires understanding the whole before writing any part — same architectural thinking as system design.

examples: Billie Jean — F#m chromatic descent creating hypnotic forward momentum
Don't Stop 'Til You Get Enough — chromatic neighbor tones in bass register
Walking bass in Count Basie arrangements
theory_nodes.description: Bass parts moving by half-steps through a progression, creating smooth voice-leading in the lowest register. A reharmonization technique from jazz arranging that Jones deployed in pop production contexts where the theoretical apparatus is invisible to the listener but structurally essential.

seattle_connection: Garfield High School, Central District, Jackson Street jazz corridor

theory_genealogy: Chromatic bass lines — first instance, signature technique
Cross-genre synthesis — first instance, defining methodology
immediate_items: Bill Frisell (degree 1) inherits: tritone substitution lineage, Central District jazz legacy, chromatic harmony vocabulary
The jazz-to-all-genres bridge Jones built should be traced forward through every subsequent artist
Jackson Street corridor context carries to all Central District degrees (0-22)
pattern_items: Seattle talent export pattern: local cultivation, global career — track this across all 360 degrees
Arranging-as-architecture principle: connect to Amiga Principle in every release where production methodology is discussed
Pedagogical chain: Jones learned from Jackson Street, taught through recordings — trace how each artist's learning lineage connects to institutional and informal education
